name: Skills for Care — State of the Sector 2025
statistic: As at 2024/25, there were 1.60 million filled posts in adult social care in England (out of 1.71 million total posts), an increase of 3.4% (52,000 filled posts) from 2023/24. The turnover rate dropped to 23.1%, equivalent to approximately 335,000 leavers per year — the lowest level for several years. There were 111,000 vacant posts, a decrease of 16,000 (12.4%) from 2023/24. The workforce contributes an estimated £77.8 billion to the economy (up 12% year-on-year) across 42,000 establishments. Additional context: the median hourly rate for care workers was £12.00 (just 56p above the National Living Wage), 45% of the workforce hold a relevant qualification, 27% are aged 55 or over, and the proportion of non-EU nationals has risen from 10% to 25% since 2021/22. International recruitment halved in the past year from 105,000 to 50,000 new joiners. Skills for Care projects that 470,000 additional posts will be needed by 2040 to match population ageing. Note: the task description states 23.7% turnover, but the published national figure is 23.1%. Regional rates vary (e.g., West Midlands at 23.4%), so 23.7% may refer to a specific sub-sector or earlier data point.
credibility: Government-affiliated. Skills for Care is the strategic workforce development body for adult social care in England, funded by the Department of Health and Social Care. Their annual State of the Sector report is the definitive source for care workforce data, cited by government, regulators (CQC), and across the sector.
applicability: Directly care sector — specifically adult social care workforce in England. Covers residential care, domiciliary care, supported living, and all regulated care settings.
citable: Yes — freely available for citation with attribution to Skills for Care. The full report, executive summary, and statistical appendix are all publicly downloadable. Standard citation: 'Skills for Care, The state of the adult social care sector and workforce in England, 2025.'
website_placement: Pain point section (workforce crisis narrative), social proof bar (headline stats: 1.6M workers, 111K vacancies, 23.1% turnover), and hero section if leading with the workforce challenge angle. The vacancy and turnover figures create urgency: 'The sector loses 335,000 workers a year — can you afford to waste their time on paperwork?' The £12.00/hour median wage stat supports the financial pressure narrative.
emotional_vs_rational: Both. The turnover and vacancy data appeal to rational decision-making (workforce cost, recruitment spend, operational risk). The low wage data (£12.00/hour, 56p above minimum wage) and the statistic that 27% of the workforce is over 55 trigger emotional responses about the value placed on care workers and the looming retirement cliff. The 335,000 leavers per year figure creates visceral urgency.
clarica_differentiator: Strong differentiator. These workforce statistics directly support Clarica's positioning: if you can't recruit enough staff, you need to make existing staff more productive — and AI is the fastest route to doing that. Clarica can frame its AI solutions as a workforce multiplier rather than a replacement: 'With 111,000 vacancies and turnover at 23.1%, your care home can't afford to lose 20 minutes per shift to paperwork.' This positions Clarica as solving a workforce crisis with AI, not just selling software features.
refresh_frequency: Annual. Published every October. The next update (covering 2025/26 data) is expected in October 2026. Skills for Care also publishes quarterly workforce survey updates via DHSC throughout the year.
comparison_pair: Year-on-year comparison: vacancies fell from 127,000 to 111,000 (12.4% drop) and turnover fell from 26.7% to 23.1% — but both remain historically high and the improvement is fragile, driven primarily by international recruitment that is now being restricted. Before/after framing: 'Even with vacancies falling 12%, there are still 111,000 unfilled posts. AI automation of admin tasks is the equivalent of adding thousands of care hours back into the system.' Longer-term comparison: the workforce needs to grow by 470,000 posts (26.9%) by 2040 to keep pace with demographic demand.
